title: Return on ad spend (ROAS)
template: SELECT campaign_id, SUM(revenue) as revenue, SUM(spend) as spend, SUM(revenue) / NULLIF(SUM(spend), 0) as roas FROM campaign_performance GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY roas DESC
anti_patterns: using attributed revenue from last-click only — overstates high-funnel campaign ROAS
dividing total company revenue by campaign spend — only revenue attributable to the campaign belongs in numerator

title: Cost per acquisition (CPA)
template: SELECT campaign_id, SUM(spend) as spend, COUNT(conversions) as conversions, SUM(spend) / NULLIF(COUNT(conversions), 0) as cpa FROM campaign_performance GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY cpa ASC
anti_patterns: mixing different conversion types (purchases, signups, leads) in the same CPA — define one conversion event per query
not deduplying conversions when multiple tracking pixels fire for the same event

title: Click-through rate (CTR) with impression floor
template: SELECT campaign_id, ad_id, SUM(impressions) as impressions, SUM(clicks) as clicks, SUM(clicks) * 1.0 / NULLIF(SUM(impressions), 0) as ctr FROM ad_performance WHERE impressions >= 1000 GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY ctr DESC
anti_patterns: reporting CTR without an impression floor — a 100% CTR on 1 impression is meaningless noise
comparing CTR across channels with structurally different impression definitions (search vs display)

title: A/B test statistical significance (z-test approximation)
template: WITH variants AS (SELECT variant, COUNT(*) as users, SUM(converted) as conversions, AVG(converted) as cvr FROM ab_test_results GROUP BY 1), stats AS (SELECT MAX(CASE WHEN variant='control' THEN cvr END) as cvr_control, MAX(CASE WHEN variant='treatment' THEN cvr END) as cvr_treatment, MAX(CASE WHEN variant='control' THEN users END) as n_control, MAX(CASE WHEN variant='treatment' THEN users END) as n_treatment FROM variants) SELECT cvr_control, cvr_treatment, (cvr_treatment - cvr_control) / NULLIF(cvr_control, 0) as relative_lift, (cvr_treatment - cvr_control) / NULLIF(SQRT(cvr_control*(1-cvr_control)/n_control + cvr_treatment*(1-cvr_treatment)/n_treatment), 0) as z_score FROM stats
anti_patterns: peeking at results before minimum sample size is reached — inflates false positive rate
z_score > 1.96 ≈ 95% confidence; z_score > 2.58 ≈ 99% — document which threshold was pre-registered

title: Campaign budget pacing
template: WITH daily AS (SELECT campaign_id, spend_date, SUM(spend) as daily_spend, budget / DATE_DIFF('day', campaign_end, campaign_start) as ideal_daily FROM campaign_spend JOIN campaigns USING(campaign_id) GROUP BY 1,2,3,4) SELECT campaign_id, spend_date, daily_spend, ideal_daily, SUM(daily_spend) OVER (PARTITION BY campaign_id ORDER BY spend_date) as cumulative_spend, SUM(ideal_daily) OVER (PARTITION BY campaign_id ORDER BY spend_date) as ideal_cumulative, SUM(daily_spend) OVER (PARTITION BY campaign_id ORDER BY spend_date) / NULLIF(SUM(ideal_daily) OVER (PARTITION BY campaign_id ORDER BY spend_date), 0) as pacing_ratio FROM daily
anti_patterns: measuring pacing by spend alone without comparing to ideal linear burn — a campaign can be within budget but severely front-loaded

title: Email campaign open, click, unsubscribe rates
template: SELECT campaign_id, COUNT(DISTINCT recipient_id) as sent, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN opened THEN recipient_id END) as opens, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN clicked THEN recipient_id END) as clicks, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN unsubscribed THEN recipient_id END) as unsubs, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN opened THEN recipient_id END) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT recipient_id), 0) as open_rate, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN clicked THEN recipient_id END) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN opened THEN recipient_id END), 0) as ctor, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN unsubscribed THEN recipient_id END) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT recipient_id), 0) as unsub_rate FROM email_events GROUP BY 1
anti_patterns: computing CTOR (click-to-open) as clicks/sent instead of clicks/opens — CTOR measures creative quality independent of subject line

title: Multi-touch attribution (linear model)
template: WITH touchpoints AS (SELECT user_id, channel, touchpoint_time, conversion_value, COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id) as touch_count FROM user_touchpoints WHERE converted = true) SELECT channel, SUM(conversion_value / touch_count) as attributed_revenue, COUNT(*) as touchpoints FROM touchpoints GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
anti_patterns: using last-click for brand campaigns and first-click for acquisition — biases the channel mix comparison

title: Reach and frequency analysis
template: SELECT campaign_id, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) as reach, SUM(impressions) as total_impressions, SUM(impressions) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT user_id), 0) as avg_frequency, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN impression_count > 10 THEN user_id END) as overexposed_users FROM user_impressions GROUP BY 1
anti_patterns: reporting total impressions without unique reach — high impressions with low reach means heavy frequency that can cause ad fatigue

title: Cohort revenue by acquisition campaign
template: WITH acq AS (SELECT user_id, campaign_id, acquisition_date FROM user_acquisition), rev AS (SELECT o.user_id, a.campaign_id, DATE_DIFF('month', a.acquisition_date, o.order_date) as months_after_acq, o.revenue FROM orders o JOIN acq a USING(user_id)) SELECT campaign_id, SUM(CASE WHEN months_after_acq = 0 THEN revenue END) / COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) as m0_ltv, SUM(CASE WHEN months_after_acq <= 3 THEN revenue END) / COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) as m3_ltv, SUM(CASE WHEN months_after_acq <= 12 THEN revenue END) / COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) as m12_ltv FROM rev GROUP BY 1
anti_patterns: comparing LTV across campaigns of different ages — newer campaigns have less time to generate revenue

title: Campaign performance by daypart / day of week
template: SELECT EXTRACT(DOW FROM event_time) as day_of_week, EXTRACT(HOUR FROM event_time) as hour, SUM(spend) as spend, SUM(conversions) as conversions, SUM(spend) / NULLIF(SUM(conversions), 0) as cpa FROM campaign_events GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY cpa ASC
anti_patterns: optimising daypart based on conversion count alone without normalising by spend — busy hours look better simply because more budget runs then
